speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. I see you've been started on insulin glargine 10 units once daily at bedtime to help manage your blood sugar levels.

speaker: doctor
text: At your last visit on November 10th, your systolic blood pressure was 157 mmHg — a bit elevated, so we’ll keep an eye on that.

speaker: doctor
text: You were diagnosed with Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus during that same visit, and we’re focusing on optimizing both glycemic and cardiovascular control.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate was elevated at 108 beats per minute that day — not alarming, but worth noting alongside your other vitals.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature was normal at 37.4°C — within the typical range.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve been taking the insulin every night as prescribed. My fasting sugars have been hovering around 130–150 mg/dL.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s a good start. How’s your energy level? Any dizziness, chest tightness, or shortness of breath?

speaker: patient
text: I’ve felt a little fatigued, and yesterday I noticed some mild chest discomfort when walking up stairs.

speaker: doctor
text: Any fever, chills, cough, or urinary symptoms? And have you checked your blood pressure at home recently?

speaker: patient
text: No cough or chills, but I did feel warm yesterday evening. I took my BP this morning — it was still 157 mmHg, same as before.

speaker: doctor
text: Thank you for tracking that. Let’s repeat your vitals now — I’d like to check your current temperature, heart rate, and blood pressure.

speaker: patient
text: Sure. I’ve also had some increased thirst over the past two days, and I’m urinating more frequently.

speaker: doctor
text: Those are classic hyperglycemia symptoms — could indicate your current regimen needs adjustment, especially if there’s an underlying stressor like infection.

speaker: patient
text: I did notice a sore throat this morning, and my glands feel swollen.

speaker: doctor
text: Let me examine your throat and listen to your lungs. Given your symptoms and vital trends, we should consider an acute illness contributing to your glucose and BP fluctuations.

speaker: patient
text: Makes sense — I’ve also been feeling quite warm all day.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, that reading from last time — the 157 mmHg — matches what I saw just now at home, so it’s consistent.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature is now 38.6°C — that confirms a low-grade fever, likely indicating an infectious process. We’ll need to address that promptly.

speaker: patient
text: Oh — I didn’t realize it had gone up that much. Is that why my blood sugar spiked?

speaker: doctor
text: Exactly — and interestingly, your repeat blood pressure is now down to 139 mmHg, likely reflecting improved vascular tone after hydration and reduced sympathetic drive. I am going to switch you to insulin glargine 12 units nightly for the next week and add acetaminophen for fever; we’ll recheck in 48 hours.
